coredata.dc:title: One-pot synthesis of nano Zr-based metal-organic frameworks for fluorescence determination of quercetin and Hg2+
coredata.dc:creator: Feng, Shitao
Tang, Furong
Wu, Fengshou
Zhang, Juan
coredata.dc:description: In this study, a nanoscale Zr-based metal–organic framework (nano-Zr-MOF) was prepared by one-pot method using meso-tetra(4-carboxyphenyl)porphyrin as organic ligand and Zr4+ as metal unit. The nanoscale structure endows it with excellent dispersion in water. The nano-Zr-MOF exhibited intense red fluorescence, which could be significantly quenched by the addition of quercetin, probably due to its electron-rich framework. The high selectivity for quercetin detection was verified with analogues and common ions as interfering agents. Moreover, the nano-Zr-MOF could be used as a highly selective and sensitive sensor for the detection of Hg2+. The detection limits for quercetin and Hg2+ were 0.026 μM and 0.039 μM, respectively. This fluorometric method was successfully applied to detect quercetin in red wine and food samples with satisfactory recoveries ranging from 83.7–112.3% and 81.8–115.9%, respectively. The recovery in detection of Hg2+ in lake water were ranging from 97.1–109.2%.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Metal-organic framework
Fluorescence detection
Quercetin
Hg2+

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Quenching mechanism of nano-Zr-MOF induced by Que

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Natural Science Foundation of Hubei Province

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 81503036 ), National Key Research and Development Program of China (No. 2022YFC3902702 ), Nature Science Foundation of Hubei Province ( 2022CFB267 ), and the Innovation Project of Key Laboratory of Novel Biomass-Based Environmental and Energy Materials in Petroleum and Chemical Industry ( 2022BEEA06 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of (A) the fabrication of the nano-Zr-MOF and (B) quenching concept of Que and Hg2+.
(A-B) TEM images, (C) full scan XPS spectrum and (D) high resolution Zr 3d spectrum of as-synthesized nano-Zr-MOF.
(A) TGA analysis, (B), PXRD patterns, (C), nitrogen adsorption–desorption isotherms and (D) FT-IR spectra for the nano-Zr-MOF.

(A) The excitation and emission spectra of nano-Zr-MOF and (B) emission spectra of nano-Zr-MOF under different excitation wavelengths from 350 to 440 nm. The effect of (C) concentration of nano-Zr-MOF, (D) ionic strength(NaCl, w/v, %), (E) pH and (F) time on the fluorescence intensity of nano-Zr-MOF.

(A) Fluorescence response of nano-Zr-MOF to different concentration of Que (0–70.0 μM) and (B) Stern-Volmer linear curves between concentration of Que and F0/F. (C) Fluorescence lifetime of nano-Zr-MOF with and without Que, (D) UV–Vis absorption of Que, nano-Zr-MOF and nano-Zr-MOF-Que, and the fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of nano-Zr-MOF-Que. (E) F0/F of nano-Zr-MOF in the presence of Que and structural analogues (10 μM) (F) F0/F of nano-Zr-MOF for different cations (100 μM) in the presence and absence of Que (10 μM). The orange columns represent F0/F of nano-Zr-MOF for different interferents. The green columns represent F0/F of nano-Zr-MOF after adding the Que (10 μM) into the mentioned above solution. Excitation wavelengths at 430 nm and emission wavelengths at 660 nm. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Comparison of the proposed fluorescence detection method for Que and Hg2+ with other reported methods.

row.entry: Tap water, distilled water and river water
Lake water, tap water and drink water

head.ce:title: One-pot synthesis of nano Zr-based metal-organic frameworks for fluorescence determination of quercetin and Hg2+

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Environmental Engineering, Hubei Key Laboratory of Novel Reactor and Green Chemical Technology, Wuhan Institute of Technology, Wuhan 430205, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Environmental Engineering
Hubei Key Laboratory of Novel Reactor and Green Chemical Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Environmental Engineering, Hubei Key Laboratory of Novel Reactor and Green Chemical Technology, Wuhan Institute of Technology, Wuhan 430205, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Engineering and Pharmacy, Key Laboratory for Green Chemical Process of Ministry of Education, Key Laboratory of Novel Biomass-Based Environmental and Energy Materials in Petroleum and Chemical Industry, Wuhan Institute of Technology, Wuhan 430205, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemical Engineering and Pharmacy
Key Laboratory for Green Chemical Process of Ministry of Education
Key Laboratory of Novel Biomass-Based Environmental and Energy Materials in Petroleum and Chemical Industry

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemical Engineering and Pharmacy, Key Laboratory for Green Chemical Process of Ministry of Education, Key Laboratory of Novel Biomass-Based Environmental and Energy Materials in Petroleum and Chemical Industry, Wuhan Institute of Technology, Wuhan 430205, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, a nanoscale Zr-based metal–organic framework (nano-Zr-MOF) was prepared by one-pot method using meso-tetra(4-carboxyphenyl)porphyrin as organic ligand and Zr4+ as metal unit. The nanoscale structure endows it with excellent dispersion in water. The nano-Zr-MOF exhibited intense red fluorescence, which could be significantly quenched by the addition of quercetin, probably due to its electron-rich framework. The high selectivity for quercetin detection was verified with analogues and common ions as interfering agents. Moreover, the nano-Zr-MOF could be used as a highly selective and sensitive sensor for the detection of Hg2+. The detection limits for quercetin and Hg2+ were 0.026 μM and 0.039 μM, respectively. This fluorometric method was successfully applied to detect quercetin in red wine and food samples with satisfactory recoveries ranging from 83.7–112.3% and 81.8–115.9%, respectively. The recovery in detection of Hg2+ in lake water were ranging from 97.1–109.2%.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Quercetin (Que) is a natural flavonoid, which is widely distributed in fruits, plants and vegetables. Que has a variety of biological activities, the most significant of which is its antioxidant activity, as well as anti-inflammatory, antiviral, antibacterial, anticancer and vasodilator effects (Liu et al., 2020; Mihaljevic et al., 2022). Recently, it has been reported that Que can prolong life (Pang et al., 2022). Therefore, it is necessary to detect Que, which will be beneficial to the research in pharmacology, biology and clinical medicine. At present, there are many methods including Raman spectroscopy, high-performance liquid chromatography and electrochemical method reported to detect Que (Numata & Tanaka, 2011; Gomez, et al., 2016). Hg2+ is one of the most common heavy metal pollutants, which can accumulate in water, plants and animals, and finally gather in the human body through the food chain, resulting in carcinogenic or teratogenic and other physiological effects on the human body (Zhang et al., 2017). Therefore, it is very important to detect the residue Hg2+ in the environment and food. Many methods including fluorescence, electrochemical and atomic spectrometric method have been widely used for detection of Hg2+ (Chen et al., 2021). Among them, fluorescence method is considered to be an effective detection technology because of its advantages of high sensitivity, high selectivity and simple operation (Luo et al., 2019). Fluorescence method has been widely used in the determination of flavonoids and heavy metal ions. Gao et al. employed high-luminescent nitrogen and phosphorus co-doped graphene-analogous flakes based on a simple one-pot method successfully used for the fluorescence detection of Que (Gao et al., 2019). Ma et al. quenched the red fluorescence of Cys-AuNP based on the interaction between Cys-AuNP and Hg2+, and then determined the target Hg2+ by fluorescence method (Ma et al., 2019).

Metal-organic framework (MOFs) refer to one-dimensional, two-dimensional or three-dimensional frameworks formed by organic bridging ligands and metal atoms through coordination bonds (Zhang et al., 2021). Because of its excellent chemical properties, unsaturated metal sites, tunable pores, and large specific surface area, MOFs have abundant applications in pollutant enrichment, gas storage and separation, catalysis, and drug delivery (Yu et al., 2023). In recent years, it has been found that some MOFs have been widely used in fluorescence sensing due to their unique optical properties to convert the chemical information/events generated by host–guest interactions into detectable luminescence changes (Jia et al., 2023). Wu et al. developed europium-MOF by simple solvothermal method and detected Fe3+ and benzaldehyde in aqueous solution with high sensitivity (Wu et al., 2021). Jia et al. prepared dual-emitting Ru@UiO-66-NH2 composite used as a ratio fluorescence sensor to detect Hg2+ in water (Jia et al., 2021). However, due to the general micron size of MOF, lack of good dispersion in aqueous solution, poor hydrolytic stability or easy channel shrinkage into condensed MOFs, sensing process of MOFs are confined (Yang et al., 2016). For the on-site detection of Que and Hg2+ in aqueous environments, it seems preferable to explore a chemically stable luminescence. At the same time, the fluorescence sensing can simply and quickly provide a variety of output signals, which makes the detection results more convincing. Due to this properties, it strongly promotes us to develop chemiluminescent MOF with good dispersibility and fluorescence response for the practical detection of Que and Hg2+.

Zr6 cluster is considered to be one of the most stable building units in the construction of MOFs due to its high porosity and resistance to a variety of solvents, and the synthesized Zr-based MOF (Zr-MOF) has excellent chemical stability and great potential in optical sensing (Peng et al., 2019). The porphyrin molecules have unique electronic and optical characteristics because of their large π-π conjugated structure. It is worth pointing out that the four pyrrole rings of TCPP form a “trap” skeleton pore, which provides a natural habitat for guest-molecules. The special properties of Zr-MOF were also illustrated by our previous studies in our group (Feng et al., 2022). In our previous study, we fabricated boronic acid grafted Zr-MOF and its magnetic material for selective enrichment of cis-diol-containing compounds. The “trap” skeleton in MOF is beneficial to capture the target analytes based on host-gust interaction. Xu et al. also designed a CDs@ZIF-8 sensor for highly sensitive capture and enrichment of Que based on this host-gust principle. The capture of guest-molecules in pores increases the opportunity of guest–host interactions and pre-centralizes guest molecules, thus improving the sensing sensitivity (Xu et al., 2016). However, the reported Zr-MOF used in fluorescence sensing show poor dispersibility in aqueous solution and easy to sedimentation due to its micron size, and the optical stability of micron Zr-MOF was also should be addressed. The synthesis of Zr-MOF with smaller size may overcome these properties.

In this study, in order to improve the dispersion and stability of Zr-MOF, the nanoscale Zr-MOF (nano-Zr-MOF) was synthesized by one-pot synthesis method with TCPP as organic ligand and Zr4+ as metal unit. The synthesized nano-Zr-MOF has excellent water dispersibility and intense fluorescence with high selectivity and sensitivity for Que and Hg2+ detection. The effects of concentration, ionic strength, pH vale and time on the fluorescence intensity of nano-Zr-MOF were evaluated. Under the optimum conditions, this fluorometric method was applied to the determination of Que in food samples and Hg2+ in lake water with high reliability and accuracy.

Epinephrine hydrochloride (≥98%), zirconium tetrachloride (ZrCl4, 98%), and catechol (AR) were provided by Aladdin Biochemical Technology (Shanghai, China). dl-norepinephrine hydrochloride (97%), resorcinol (AR), hydroquinone (AR) and benzoic acid (≥99%) were supplied by Lingfeng Chemical Reagent (Shanghai, China). Apigenin (98%), Kaempferol (98%), meso-tetra (4-carboxyphenyl) porphyrin(TCPP) was supplied by Energy Chemical (Shanghai, China). Serotonin hydrochloride (99%) was purchased from Bide Pharmatech (Shanghai, China). Acetic acid (HAc), methanol (MeOH, AR), sodium acetate anhydrous (NaAc, AR) and N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF, AR) were provided by Sinopharm Chemical Reagent (Shanghai, China). Dopamine hydrochloride (98%) was purchased from Geao Chemical Technology (Wuhan, China). Que (97%) was purchased from Meryer Chemical Technology (Shanghai, China). All reagents were used without further purification. Ultrapure water was purified by a Milli-Q system (MA, USA). Great wall red wine, tea (Biluochun, Longjin, Jasmine tea), apples, oranges, pears and red onions were bought from Wal-Mart supermarket.

Fluorescence lifetime measurements were conducted with the lifetime spectrometer （FLS1000, Edinburgh). Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) particular surface area and pore volume were determined using nitrogen adsorption–desorption (Micromeritics ASAP 2460, USA). Spectrum Two Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrometer were used to collect the FT-IR spectra (PerkinElmer, UK). On a thermogravimetric analyzer, the thermal gravimetric analysis was recorded from ambient temperature to 800 °C under an N2 environment at a rate of 10 °C min−1 (TGA5500, USA). On an X-ray diffractometer, patterns of powder X-ray diffraction (PXRD) were recorded with Bruker D8 advance (Germany). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was carried out on ESCALAB Xi + spectrometer (Thermo, USA). The ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis) was recorded on a Shimadzu (Japan) UV–Vis spectrophotometer. The fluorescence measurements were carried out with FluoroMax-4 (Horiba, Japan) fluorescence spectrometer. The morphology of the prepared nano-Zr-MOF was characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (JEM2100, Japan).

The nano-Zr-MOF is synthesized after slight modification based on the reported method (Park et al., 2016). The synthesis process of nano-Zr-MOF is shown in Fig. 1 A. ZrCl4 (75 mg, 0.32 mmol), TCPP (30 mg, 0.038 mmol), and benzoic acid (840 mg, 6.9 mmol) were dissolved in 30 mL of DMF in a 100 mL round bottom flask. The mixture was stirred (300 rpm) at 90 °C for 5 h. After the reaction was completed, nano-Zr-MOF were collected by centrifugation (12000 g, 20 min) and washed with fresh DMF and acetone until it was colorless. Finally, the nano-Zr-MOF was dried under vacuum at 60 °C. The resulting nano-Zr-MOF were dispersed in ultrapure water with concentration of 1 mg mL−1 for storage at 5 °C in refrigerator.

A certain amount of Que was dissolved in methanol to prepare 10 mM standard solution. The standard solution was diluted into a series of different concentrations (10 μM-10 mM) with ultrapure water. In the fluorescence experiment, the nano-Zr-MOF of 1 mg mL−1 was dispersed uniformly by ultrasound before use. A series of 30 μL of Que with different concentrations was respectively mixed with 60 μL of nano-Zr-MOF dispersion solution, and was further diluted to 3 mL with ultrapure water. The fluorescence spectroscopy was used to determine the fluorescence intensity of each solution before (F0 ) and after (F) adding Que. The preparation of Hg2+ stock solution with different concentration (10 μM-10 mM) is the same as that of Que, except that ultrapure water was chosen as the solvent. The quenching concept of Que and Hg2+ is shown in Fig. 1B. In addition, under the excitation of 430 nm, the fluorescence spectra were recorded, with the emission ranging from 600 to 780 nm. The width of both excitation and emission slits is 5 nm. After three parallel scans, each spectrum was obtained. According to the Stern-Volmer equation, the fluorescence quenching efficiency was quantitatively evaluated as follows (Cai et al., 2020):
(1)
F 0
F
=
K
SV
C + 1
where F0 represents the fluorescence intensity of nano-Zr-MOF before adding the target analyte, F represents the fluorescence intensity of nano-Zr-MOF after adding the target analyte, C (μmol L-1) is the target analyte concentration and KSV (L μmol-1) is the quenching coefficient.

Red wine was bought from supermarket for the real sample analysis. Before analysis, the wine was filtered with a 0.22 μm filter membrane to remove possible impurities from the solution, and stored in a refrigerator at 4 °C. The 30 μL of prepared sample solution was mixed with 60 μL of 1 mg mL−1 nano-Zr-MOF dispersion solution, and the mixture was diluted to 3 mL with HAc-NaAc buffer (pH = 7) for final fluorescence detection. The recoveries were studied by standard spiking with three different concentrations of Que (1, 5 and 20 μM) to verify the practical application of the established method.

Several foods including fruits and vegetables were also selected as real samples for analysis and determination of Que in it. Tea, apples, oranges, pears and red onions are bought from the supermarket. For tea samples preparation, 0.10 g of tea samples were weighed and mixed with 10 mL of hot water (80 °C). After extraction of 20 min, the tea extracts were filtered by 0.22 μm filter membrane and stored in a refrigerator at 4 °C. For fruit juice, 40 g of fruit fleshes were weighed and mixed with 50 mL of ultrapure water, homogenized with a juicer and centrifuged at 12000 rpm for 5 min, juice was filtered by 0.22 μm filter and stored in a refrigerator at 4 °C. Apple peel, pear peel, orange peel, red onion peel and red onion flesh were respectively cut into pieces and dried in an oven at 50 °C for 3 days. Then, 0.10 g of the samples were weighed and soaked in 10 mL of methanol shaking for 24 h, the extracts were filtered by 0.22 μm filter membrane and stored in a refrigerator at 4 °C. The operations about the Que detection were the same as section 2.4, and the recoveries were studied by standard spiking three different concentrations of Que (1, 5 and 20 μM) to verify the practical application of the detection method of Que.

Lake water was chosen as real sample for evaluation and determination of heavy metal residue. The lake water was taken from the JingXin Lake in Wuhan Institute of Technology. It was filtered with a 0.22 μm filter membrane to remove the impurities present in the lake water. The operation was the same as in section 2.4 except that Hg2+ stock solution was prepared with lake water. The recoveries were studied by standard spiking three different concentrations of Hg2+ (1, 5 and 20 μM) to verify the practical application of the method.

The morphological characterization of the synthesized nano-Zr-MOF was performed by TEM. It can be seen from Fig. 2 A-B that most of the synthesized nano-Zr-MOF presented in block shape and a few were in a rod structure. This was due to the different connectivity of Zr6 clusters (Yu et al., 2021), which leaded to the mixed morphology of particles. The average size of the block structure is 100 nm. The morphology indicated the successful synthesis of nano-Zr-MOF.

The element composition of nano-Zr-MOF was analyzed by XPS. Fig. 2C showed that there are Zr, C, O, and N elements in nano-Zr-MOF. As illustrated in Fig. 2D, the Zr 3d5/2 and Zr 3d3/2 of nano-Zr-MOF are located in 182.1 eV and 184.5 eV, respectively, which are consistent with those reported in other literatures (Zhou et al., 2022). The spectra of O 1 s (Fig. S1) contained two different peaks ascribed to Zr-O and CO at binding energy 529.4 eV and 531.1 eV, respectively. The above characterization also shows that nano-Zr-MOF has been synthesized successfully.

The thermal stability of nano-Zr-MOF was evaluated by TGA. As shown in Fig. 3 A, the TGA curves exhibited two weight loss processes, the weight loss is 18.3% before 420 °C, which can be attributed to the evaporation of the solvent residue in the pores due to the large specific surface area of nano-Zr-MOF. The weight of nano-Zr-MOF after 420 °C decreased sharply with a weight loss of about 56%, probably due to the decomposition of TCPP linkers and skeletal collapse of nano-Zr-MOF. The TGA analysis results show the nano-Zr-MOF possesses excellent thermal stability under 420 °C.

The crystallinity and purity of nano-Zr-MOF were revealed by PXRD. As shown in Fig. 3B, high-intensity diffraction peaks are shown at 4.6° (002), 6.5° (022), 8.0° (222), 9.2° (004), 11.3° (224) and 13.8° (006), which are consistent with those reported in other literatures (Jin et al., 2021), indicating the excellent crystallinity of nano-Zr-MOF. These diffraction peaks also show that most of the synthesized nano-Zr-MOF are block shape, which is consistent with the results of TEM.

The porosity of nano-Zr-MOF was measured by N2 isothermal adsorption–desorption at 77 K. As can be seen from Fig. 3C, the BET surface area of nano-Zr-MOF was 2065.6 m2 g−1. Moreover, the adsorption isotherm represents a reversible adsorption behavior of type I, which is usually a single molecular layer adsorption, reflecting microporous property. As shown in the inset of Fig. 3C, the micropore characteristic was further reflected and verified. The total pore volume and t-Plot micropore volume were 1.01 cm3 g−1 and 0.57 cm3 g−1, respectively, with an average pore diameter of 1.95 nm.

The FT-IR spectra of nano-Zr-MOF was shown in Fig. 3D. The peak at 1702 cm−1 attributed to the CO stretching vibration, and the peaks at 1604 cm−1 and 1542 cm−1 attributed to the skeleton vibration of benzene ring (Feng et al., 2022). Two peaks at 1416 cm−1 and 1182 cm−1 corresponded to the stretching vibration of CO in the carboxyl group (Li et al., 2022a). The stretching vibration of CN bond of pyrrole ring (Feng et al., 2020) indicated the successful involvement of TCPP in the MOF synthesis. In addition, the stretching vibration at 3420 cm−1 corresponded to –OH (Chen et al., 2019). The unique absorption peak at 488 cm−1 representing the asymmetric stretching vibration of Zr-O (Feng et al., 2022) indicated the successful participation of Zr in the synthesis. In summary, the results show that nano-Zr-MOF has been successfully prepared.

The nano-Zr-MOF exhibited intense red luminescence under UV light (365 nm) irradiation. As shown in Fig. 4 A, the optimal excitation and emission wavelengths were located at 430 nm and 660 nm, respectively. The emission spectra of nano-Zr-MOF under different excitation wavelengths were shown in Fig. 4B, the fluorescence intensity of nano-Zr-MOF first increased and then decreased with the excitation wavelength increasing from 350 nm to 440 nm, the optimal emission wavelength remained basically unchanged, which indicated the non-excitation wavelength-dependent fluorescence behavior.

Meanwhile, the effects of concentration, ionic strength (NaCl, w/v, %), pH value and time on the fluorescence intensity of nano-Zr-MOF were investigated. As shown in Fig. 4C-F, the optimal concentration of nano-Zr-MOF was 0.02 μg mL−1, and the optimal ionic strength was 0%. When the pH value was between 7 and 9, nano-Zr-MOF showed the highest fluorescence intensity. The possible reason is that the protonation of nitrogen in four pyrrole rings in MOF under acidic conditions presents electron-withdrawing capability and weakens the conjugated system of porphyrin. These properties result in an increase in the energy levels of electronic transitions on the porphyrin macrocycle, leading to a decrease in the fluorescence intensity of the nano-Zr-MOF. For the subsequent experiments, the pH value of the solution was chosen to be 7. The fluorescence intensity of nano-Zr-MOF did not show any significant variation during the storage for 7 days, indicating its high colloidal stability.

The application of the obtained nano-Zr-MOF in chemo-sensing was verified with Que as targeted analyte, a typical flavonoid compound. As the reaction time might affect the quenching results, we investigated the effect of response time on the fluorescence change (F0/F). Specifically, the F0/F value of nano-Zr-MOF at different times was recorded at the given Que concentration (10 μM). As shown in Fig. S2, F 0 /F reached the maximum value after incubation for 1 min and remained stable in the subsequent 11 min, indicating the quick response of nano-Zr-MOF toward Que. The fluorescence intensity of nano-Zr-MOF decreased gradually with the increase of Que concentration from 0.1 to 70 μM (Fig. 5 A). As shown in Fig. 5B, the fluorescence change (F0/F) presented a good linear relationship with the Que concentrations in the range of 0.1–5 μM and 5–70 μM. Under irradiation of UV light (365 nm), the bright red fluorescence of nano-Zr-MOF gradually quenched with the increase of Que concentration, indicating the feasibility of Que detection through naked eyes. The KSV values of Que derived from the Stern-Volmer equation were calculated to be 0.09341 in the range of 0.1–5 μM, and 0.03362 in range of 5–70 μM with good correlation coefficients (0.9984 and 0.9905, respectively). Based on the slope of the corresponding linear curve (0.1–5 μM), the detection limit (LOD) of Que was valued as 0.026 μM (S/N = 3).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quenching mechanism of nano-Zr-MOF induced by Que
ce:section.ce:para: To demonstrate the quenching mechanism of nano-Zr-MOF induced by Que, the fluorescence lifetime and UV–Vis absorption spectra of nano-Zr-MOF in the presence or absence of Que were studied. As shown in Fig. 5C, the average fluorescence lifetime of nano-Zr-MOF was decreased from 0.869 ns to 0.356 ns after adding Que, indicating the occurrence of photoinduced electron transfer (PET) between fluorophore and analytes (Fan et al., 2021). Moreover, the absorption peaks of both Que and nano-Zr-MOF at 365 nm and 425 nm were significantly red-shifted with addition of Que to the aqueous solution of nano-Zr-MOF (Fig. 5D), indicating the formation of complex between Que and nano-Zr-MOF due to the strong interaction. Based on the “hard and soft acids and bases theory” (HSAB) (Wu and Chen, 2014), the phenolic hydroxyl structures of Que (as soft acids) might interact with the nitrogen-containing groups of nano-Zr-MOF (as soft bases) to form nano-Zr-MOF-Que complex. The complex may prevent the transfer of non-radiative electrons from hole and weaken the release of recombination fluorescence, resulting in static quenching (Zou et al., 2015). In addition, there was no overlap between emission spectrum of nano-Zr-MOF and absorption spectrum of Que, while partial overlap between absorption spectrum of Que and excitation spectrum of nano-Zr-MOF (Fig. 5D), indicating the inner filter effect (IFE) rather than fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) might induce the fluorescence quenching (Cai et al., 2020). Therefore, the fluorescence quenching of nano-Zr-MOF by Que was caused by the synergistic effect of static quenching, PET and IFE.
To evaluate the selectivity of nano-Zr-MOF toward Que, the structural analogues and common cations and anions was studied as interfering agents.

Firstly, the fluorescence intensity of nano-Zr-MOF was recorded with the addition of phenols (hydroquinone, resorcinol, catechol), neurotransmitters (serotonin, dopamine, epinephrine, dl-norepinephrine), and flavonoids (apigenin and kaempferol) (both 10 μM). As displayed in Fig. 5E, comparing with the same concentration (10 μM) of other structural analogues, Que presented the more obvious quenching effect on the fluorescence of nano-Zr-MOF. A slight quenching efficiency of some structural analogues for fluorescence of nano-Zr-MOF probably due to the negligible overlap between excitation spectrum of nano-Zr-MOF and their absorption spectrum resulting in weak IFE (Wu et al., 2021).

Meanwhile, the common cations (Al3+, Ca2+, Co2+, Cu2+, K+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Na+, NH4 +, Pb2+, Sn2+, Zn2+, Fe3+, Hg2+ (100 μM)) and anions (Cl-, NO3 –, NO2 –, SO4 2-, HCO3 –, HPO4 2-, H2PO4 - (100 μM)) were selected to further verify the anti-interference ability of nano-Zr-MOF. As shown in Fig. 5F and Fig. S3, even the concentration of interfering ions was 10 times higher than that of Que, they did not affect the fluorescence quenching of Que toward nano-Zr-MOF except for Hg2+. Therefore, the nano-Zr-MOF showed high selectivity and good anti-interference ability to the detection of Que. Since Hg2+ alone had a significant quenching effect on the fluorescence of nano-Zr-MOF, the relationship between Hg2+ concentration and fluorescence intensity of nano-Zr-MOF was further explored. As shown in Fig. S4, two dose-dependent interactions (0.1–10 μM and 10–100 μM) were observed between F0/F and Hg2+ concentration. The KSV values of Hg2+ derived from the equation were calculated to be 0.19743 (0.1–10 μM) and 0.02732 (10–100 μM) with the good correlation coefficients (0.9960 and 0.9978, respectively), and the LOD of Hg2+ was valued as 0.039 μM (S/N = 3).

In order to verify the practical application of nano-Zr-MOF, three different concentrations of Que (1, 5 and 20 μM) were added to the red wine sample for fluorescence detection. As shown in Table S1, the satisfactory recovery rate was in the range of 83.7–112.3% and intra-day RSD was in the range of 1.2–1.7%. The reproducibility of the method for the detection of Que in different days was investigated. Besides, the reproducibility of the method by using different batches synthesized nano-Zr-MOF was also investigated. As shown in Table S2, the inter-day RSDs and batch-to-batch RSDs in detection of Que is less than 4.1% and 4.6%, respectively. The results demonstrated the high reliability and accuracy of this method for Que detection in practical samples.

In order to further illustrate the feasibility of this method to detect Que in real samples, tea, fruits and vegetables were selected and analyzed. As shown in Table S3, the contents of Que in Biluochun tea, Longjing tea and Jasmine tea were 20.0 μM, 21.5 μM and 20.3 μM respectively, and the recoveries of Que at three different spiked concentrations ranged from 82.7 to 115.3%, with RSDs of 0.4––2.9%. However, Que was not directly detected in orange juice, pear juice and apple juice, which may be caused by the low content of Que in the juice. The range of recoveries for spiked in fruit juice was 85.4–115.9%, and the RSD was 0.3–2.8%. The contents of Que detected in pear peel, apple peel, onion peel and onion flesh were 3.2 μM, 3.1 μM, 12.4 μM and 6.3 μM respectively, and the recoveries were between 81.8 and 113.2%, and the RSD was between 0.1% and 4.5%. The results show that this method is feasible to detect Que in real samples.

In order to confirm the feasibility of the as-prepared nano-Zr-MOF for the determination of Hg2+ in real samples, three different concentrations of Hg2+ (1, 5 and 20 μM) were spiked for quantitative analysis in lake water. Table S1 and Table S2 showed that the recoveries of Hg2+ was in the range of 97.1–109.2%. The intra-day, inter-day and batch-to-batch RSDs in detection of Hg2+ is less than 1.6%, 1.7% and 3.9%, respectively, suggesting the good reproducibility of the proposed method, which is promising in analysis of real water samples.

The analytical performance of the proposed method for the detection of Que and Hg2+ was also compared with other reported articles. As shown in Table 1 , the LODs obtained from our method are relatively lower and the linear range is wider, comparing with that of reported methods. Therefore, the fluorescence sensor based on nano-Zr-MOF is of great value for the detection of Que and Hg2+ in real samples with good accuracy and high precision.

In this work, nano-Zr-MOF was synthesized by hydrothermal method with good dispersibility and distinct fluorescence. The intense red fluorescence of nano-Zr-MOF could be significantly quenched upon the addition of Que. The mechanism experiment verified the synergistic effects of static quenching, PET and IFE caused the fluorescence quenching of nano-Zr-MOF. In addition, Hg2+ alone had a significant quenching effect on the fluorescence of nano-Zr-MOF. Based on the selective fluorescence quenching of nano-Zr-MOF, a facile fluorometric method was developed for the detection of Que and Hg2+, with the LOD calculated to 0.026 μM and 0.039 μM respectively. Finally, the established method based on nano-Zr-MOF was successfully applied to detect the content of Que in food samples with recoveries ranging from 81.8% to 115.9% with RSDs lower than 4.6 %. The recoveries in lake water for detection of Hg2+ is ranging from 97.1% to 109.2% with RSDs lower than 3.9%. All these results illustrated that the established fluorescence method based on nano-Zr-MOF is feasible and reliable for real sample analysis.

Shitao Feng: Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Furong Tang: Methodology, Data curation. Fengshou Wu: Conceptualization, Writing – review & editing. Juan Zhang: Conceptualization, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 81503036), National Key Research and Development Program of China (No. 2022YFC3902702), Nature Science Foundation of Hubei Province (2022CFB267), and the Innovation Project of Key Laboratory of Novel Biomass-Based Environmental and Energy Materials in Petroleum and Chemical Industry (2022BEEA06).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodchem.2023.137173.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
